Page 3 – Warm-Up: Identify the Derivative Graph

Warm-Up – Choosing the Graph of \(f'\) from the Graph of \(f\)

Top panel: A red curve labeled “This is the graph of a function, \(f\).” The curve has one visible local maximum (a peak near \(x pprox -1\)) and one local minimum (a trough near \(x pprox 1\)), creating an S-like shape. The curve rises steeply on the right for large \(x\). Bottom panel: Six blue candidate graphs are arranged in a 2×3 grid, each showing a possible \(f'\). The candidates vary in the number, location, and direction of their zeros and extrema:
  • Some candidates have zeros at two locations (matching the two critical points of \(f\)).
  • Correct derivative must be positive where \(f\) is increasing and negative where \(f\) is decreasing.
  • The selected answer (filled circle) appears to be the graph that is negative between the two critical points and positive outside — consistent with \(f\) decreasing between its local max and min.

Problem: Given the red graph of \(f\), choose the graph of its derivative \(f'\) from the six blue options.

Key rules for reading \(f'\) from \(f\):

  • Where \(f\) has a local maximum or local minimum: \(f' = 0\) (zero crossing).
  • Where \(f\) is increasing (slope positive): \(f' > 0\).
  • Where \(f\) is decreasing (slope negative): \(f' < 0\).
  • Where \(f\) is steepest: \(|f'|\) is largest.
  • Where \(f\) has an inflection point (concavity changes): \(f'\) has a local extremum.

Apply these rules to narrow down the six options to the one whose zero-crossings align with the critical points of \(f\) and whose sign matches the intervals of increase/decrease.

Page 4 – Recall: Quantities, Covariation, and Derivatives

Review – Quantities, Covariation, and the Derivative

Black slide with white handwritten text. Key phrases visible: “Recall / Quantities are every[where in] nature. Day to day and disciplinary.” Below: “We have two quantities that vary together / co-vary — independent [and] dependent.” Final line: “If y = f(t), then f’(t) = y’ tells us how much y changes when we change t.”

Recall: Quantities are everywhere in nature — in daily life and across academic disciplines.

When two quantities vary together we label one independent (\(t\)) and the other dependent (\(y\)). The relationship is written \(y = f(t)\).

The derivative \(f'(t) = y'\) measures how much \(y\) changes per unit change in \(t\) — the instantaneous rate of change. This is the central quantity in differential equations.

Page 5 – Quantitative Situations and ODEs: Exponential Growth/Decay

From Situations to Differential Equations

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Quantitative situations give us relationships between y and its derivatives.” Below: “Examples: Exponential Growth/Decay — when change [is] proportional to itself.”

Real-world quantitative situations naturally produce relationships between a function and its derivatives. These are differential equations.

Exponential Growth / Decay: When the rate of change of \(y\) is proportional to \(y\) itself:

\[ y' = ky \]
  • \(k > 0\): exponential growth (e.g., unconstrained population, compound interest).
  • \(k < 0\): exponential decay (e.g., radioactive decay, drug metabolism).

The solution is \(y(t) = Ce^{kt}\) for an arbitrary constant \(C\).

Page 6 – Logistic Growth

Logistic Growth

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Logistic Growth. When exponential for small y but has carrying capacity N.” The equation \(y' = ky(1 - y/N)\) is partially written.

Logistic growth models a population that grows exponentially when small but is limited by a carrying capacity \(N\):

\[ y' = ky\left(1 - \frac{y}{N} \right) \]

Analysis of the factor \( (1-\frac{y}{N})\):

  • When \(y \ll N\): factor \(\approx 1\), so \( y' \approx ky\) (pure exponential growth).
  • When \(y = N\): factor \(= 0\), so \(y' = 0\) (growth stops).
  • When \(y > N\): factor \(< 0\), so \(y' < 0\) (population decreases back toward \(N\)).

Equilibria: The logistic ODE has two constant (equilibrium) solutions: \(y = 0\) (unstable) and \(y = N\) (stable).

Page 7 – Mass-Spring System Setup

Mass-Spring System – Setup

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Mass-Spring System. x(t) be the displacement in meters + seconds [after pulling]. Real world → Restorative force pulling mass back to equilibrium (Hooke’s Law). F = −kx, k > 0.”

Setup: Let \(x(t)\) = displacement of the mass in meters at time \(t\) seconds after release.

Physics: The spring exerts a restorative force opposing displacement (Hooke’s Law):

\[ F = -kx, \quad k > 0 \]

The negative sign ensures the force always points toward equilibrium: if \(x > 0\) (mass to the right), then \(F < 0\) (force to the left), and vice versa.

Page 8 – Applying Newton's Second Law to the Spring

Deriving the ODE via Newton’s Second Law

Black slide with handwritten text: “Newton: F = ma = mx”” (where the double prime denotes second derivative). Below: “So mx” = −kx.”

Newton’s second law states \(F = ma\). Since acceleration is the second derivative of position, \(a = x''(t)\), we have:

\[ F = mx'' \]

Combining with Hooke’s Law \(F = -kx\):

\[ mx'' = -kx \]

Standard form:

\[ x'' + \frac{k}{m}x = 0 \]

This is a second-order, linear, homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients. It governs simple harmonic motion.

Page 9 – Definitions: Differential Equations and Order

Differential Equations – Formal Definitions

Black slide with white handwritten text. Two bullet-style definitions: (1) “A [differential] equation is an equation relating a quantity (function) and its derivatives.” (2) “The [order] of a differential equation is the highest derivative appearing in the equation.”

Definition – Differential Equation: A differential equation is an equation that relates an unknown function to one or more of its derivatives.

Definition – Order: The order of a differential equation is the order of the highest derivative appearing in the equation.

Examples by order:

  • First-order: \(y' = -ky\)  (highest derivative: \(y'\))
  • First-order: \(y' = -ky(1-y/N)\)  (highest derivative: \(P'\))
  • Second-order: \(mx'' = -kx\)  (highest derivative: \(x''\))
  • Second-order: \(y'' - 14y' + 40y = 0\)  (highest derivative: \(y''\))

Page 10 – Solutions to ODEs

Solutions to ODEs

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Given a differential equation, a solution is any function that satisfies it. Note: There need not be any [solutions] and there may be many.”

Definition – Solution: Given a differential equation, a solution is any function that satisfies the equation identically (i.e., when substituted, both sides are equal for all values in some interval).

Important notes:

  • A differential equation may have no solutions.
  • A differential equation may have infinitely many solutions (a family parametrized by arbitrary constants).
  • To verify a solution: substitute the function and all required derivatives into the equation and check that both sides agree.

Page 11 – Initial Value Problems (IVPs)

Initial Value Problems (IVPs)

Black slide with white handwritten text: “An initial value problem (IVP) is an ODE with some initial conditions. Note: Typically you need as many initial conditions as the order [of the ODE].”

Definition – Initial Value Problem (IVP): An initial value problem consists of an ODE together with initial conditions that specify the value of the solution (and/or its derivatives) at a specific point \(t_0\).

Key principle: For an \(n\)th-order ODE, you typically need exactly \(n\) initial conditions to determine a unique particular solution.

  • 1st-order ODE (e.g., \(y' = ky\)) needs 1 initial condition: \(y(t_0) = y_0\)
  • 2nd-order ODE (e.g., \(mx'' = -kx\)) needs 2 initial conditions: \(x(t_0) = x_0\) and \(x'(t_0) = v_0\)

Page 12 – General Solution vs. Particular Solution

General Solution vs. Particular Solution – Calculus Refresher

Black slide with handwritten text and equations. The ODE “y = y” (meaning y’ = y) is written at top. Below: solutions \(f(t) = e^t\) or \(Ce^t\) are listed, labeled “Infinite family” on the left and “f(0) = C” on the right. The terms “General Solution” and “Particular Solution” are labeled beneath.

ODE: \(y' = y\)

Verification: If \(y = Ce^t\), then \(y' = Ce^t = y\). ✓

General Solution (all solutions, parametrized by the arbitrary constant \(C\)):

\[ y = Ce^t, \quad C \in \mathbb{R} \]

There are infinitely many solutions — one for each value of \(C\).

Applying an initial condition: Suppose \(y(0) = 3\). Then:

\[ Ce^0 = 3 \implies C = 3 \]

Particular Solution (unique solution satisfying the initial condition):

\[ y = 3e^t \]

Page 13 – Practice Problem: Finding Values of k

Practice Problem – Finding Values of \(k\)

White-background problem card on a dark slide. The problem reads: “Find the two values of \(k\) for which \(y(x) = e^{kx}\) is a solution of the differential equation \(y'' - 14y' + 40y = 0\).” Two labeled answer boxes appear below: “smaller value =” and “larger value =” (both blank, to be filled in).

Problem: Find the two values of \(k\) for which \(y(x) = e^{kx}\) is a solution of:

\[ y'' - 14y' + 40y = 0 \]

Smaller value = ?    Larger value = ?

Try it yourself before viewing the solution on the next page!

Page 14 – Solution: Characteristic Equation Method

Solution – Characteristic Equation Method

Black slide with white handwritten algebra. Steps shown: write \(y = e^{kx}\), compute \(y' = ke^{kx}\) and \(y'' = k^2 e^{kx}\); substitute into the ODE to get \(e^{kx}(k^2 - 14k + 40) = 0\); since \(e^{kx} eq 0\), conclude \(k^2 - 14k + 40 = 0\).

Step 1 – Compute derivatives of the trial solution \(y = e^{kx}\):

\[ y = e^{kx}, \quad y' = ke^{kx}, \quad y'' = k^2 e^{kx} \]

Step 2 – Substitute into the ODE \(y'' - 14y' + 40y = 0\):

\[ k^2 e^{kx} - 14ke^{kx} + 40e^{kx} = 0 \]

Step 3 – Factor out \(e^{kx} \neq 0\):

\[ e^{kx} (k^2-14k+40)=0 \implies k^2-14k+40=0 \]

Step 4 – Solve the characteristic equation:

\[ (k - 4)(k - 10) = 0 \implies k = 4 \mbox{ or } k = 10 \]

Answers: smaller value \(= \mathbf{4}\), larger value \(= \mathbf{10}\).

Both \(y = e^{4x}\) and \(y = e^{10x}\) satisfy the ODE. The general solution is:

\[ y = C_1 e^{4x} + C_2 e^{10x} \]

Page 15 – Slope Fields: Definition and Concept

Slope Fields (Direction Fields)

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Slope Fields. If you have a first order ODE, solve for [the] first derivative [to get] y’ = f(t, y). This gives slopes at every point. [A] solution at value (t₀, y₀) must have slope f(t₀, y₀).”

Definition – Slope Field: For a first-order ODE written in the form

\[ y' = f(t, y) \]

the slope field (or direction field) is the collection of short line segments drawn at a grid of points \((t, y)\), each with slope \(f(t, y)\).

Key insight: Any solution \(y = \phi(t)\) passing through a point \((t_0, y_0)\) must have slope equal to \(f(t_0, y_0)\) at that point. The slope field therefore shows, at a glance, the direction a solution curve must travel everywhere in the plane.

How to read a slope field:

  • Horizontal segments: \(f(t,y) = 0\) — these points form the nullcline.
  • Steep segments: large \(|f(t,y)|\).
  • Consistent direction patterns reveal the qualitative behavior of all solutions.

Page 16 – Slope Field Example: y' = -3t

Slope Field Example – \(y' = -3t\)

Black slide with handwritten text: “Ex: y’ = f(t,y) = −3t. [Plot] some of these slopes. [→] Slope Field.” The word “Slope Field” is written with an arrow indicating a diagram would follow.

ODE: \(y' = f(t, y) = -3t\)

Note: the right-hand side depends only on \(t\), not on \(y\). The slope at any grid point \((t, y)\) is simply \(-3t\), regardless of the height \(y\).

Slope pattern:

  • At \(t = 0\): slope \(= 0\) for every \(y\) — all segments along the vertical axis are horizontal.
  • At \(t = 1\): slope \(= -3\) — steep downward segments.
  • At \(t = -1\): slope \(= 3\) — steep upward segments.
  • At \(t = 0.5\): slope \(= -1.5\) — moderately downward.

Segments at the same \(t\)-value are parallel (same slope), creating horizontal stripes of parallel segments in the slope field.

The general solution can be found by integration: \(y = - frac{3}{2}t^2 + C\).

Page 17 – Slope Fields and IVPs: Solutions Follow Tangent Lines

Slope Fields and IVPs – Solutions Follow Tangent Lines

Black slide with white handwritten text: “Slope Fields and IVPs. Big Idea: A solution follows its tangent lines.” Additional handwritten annotation appears below.

Big Idea: A solution curve to a first-order ODE is tangent to the slope field at every point it passes through. In other words, the solution follows its tangent lines through the field.

Sketch procedure for an IVP \(y' = f(t,y)\), \(y(t_0) = y_0\):

  1. Plot the slope field by evaluating \(f(t,y)\) at a grid of points.
  2. Locate the initial point \((t_0, y_0)\).
  3. From \((t_0, y_0)\), move in the direction of the local segment.
  4. At each new point, realign with the local segment and continue.
  5. The resulting curve is an approximate solution to the IVP.

This graphical technique is valuable because it reveals solution behavior even when an explicit formula cannot be found.

Page 18 – Practice: Nullcline and Direction Field for dy/dt = 1/(-2t-5)

Practice – Nullcline and Direction Field for \(\dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{-2t-5}\)

White-background problem card on a dark slide. The ODE \( \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1}{-2t-5}\) is displayed at the top. Two candidate direction fields are shown side by side, each consisting of a 5×5 grid of red dashed line segments on white coordinate axes:
  • Left candidate (empty circle — incorrect): A vertical line divides the grid. To the left of the vertical line, the dashes slope upward to the right (positive slope); to the right of the line, they slope downward to the right (negative slope). The vertical line is centered in the window.
  • Right candidate (filled blue circle — correct): Same structure, but the vertical dividing line (nullcline/asymptote) is positioned to the left of center, consistent with the asymptote at \(t = -5/2 = -2.5\). To the left of the line, dashes slope upward (slope > 0); to the right, dashes slope downward (slope < 0). As \(|t|\) increases away from \(-2.5\), the dashes become more horizontal (slope approaches 0).

Problem: Which of the two direction fields correctly represents \(\dfrac{dy}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{-2t-5}\)?

Analysis:

Step 1 – Find where the slope is zero (nullcline):
\(\frac{1}{-2t-5} = 0\) has no solution (a fraction equals zero only if its numerator is zero, which it isn’t here). There is no \(t\)-nullcline (no horizontal segments anywhere).

Step 2 – Find the vertical asymptote:
The slope is undefined when \(-2t - 5 = 0\), i.e., \(t = - frac{5}{2} = -2.5\). This vertical line divides the \(t\)-axis into two regions.

Step 3 – Determine the sign of \(dy/dt\) in each region:

  • For \(t < -2.5\): \(-2t - 5 > 0\), so \(dy/dt > 0\) — segments slope upward.
  • For \(t > -2.5\): \(-2t - 5 < 0\), so \(dy/dt < 0\) — segments slope downward.

Step 4 – Determine how slope magnitude varies:
As \(t o -2.5\) from either side, \(|dy/dt| o \infty\) (very steep segments near the asymptote). As \(|t| o \infty\), \(|dy/dt| o 0\) (nearly horizontal segments far from the asymptote).

Step 5 – Note the slope is independent of \(y\):
Segments at the same \(t\) are parallel for all values of \(y\).

Correct answer: The right candidate (filled circle), where the vertical asymptote is at \(t = -2.5\) (left of center), segments to the left slope upward, and segments to the right slope downward with magnitude decreasing away from the asymptote.
